_meta.description: Differentiation strategies: emergent, ELL, extending for each topic

strategies: Use small quantities (start with 3-5 objects) before increasing to 10.
Provide objects to touch and move as students count (rote practice with physical movement).
Use number line posters with 0-10 clearly marked in the classroom.
accommodations: Allow one-on-one counting practice with manipulatives at the student's pace.
Provide large, easy-to-grab objects instead of small ones.
Use finger counting and other concrete strategies without pressure to move to abstract methods.

strategies: Use visual number cards (0-10) with pictures of objects to reinforce counting.
Provide bilingual counting resources if available.
Practice counting in context: count fingers, count steps, count classroom objects.
Challenge students to count backward from 10 to 0.
Ask students to identify 'one more' and 'one less' for numbers 0-10.
Introduce counting by 2s and 5s with skip-counting songs.
connections: Connect counting to real-world situations: 'Count the children at our table.'
Introduce subitizing: recognize quantities without counting (showing 3 fingers quickly).

strategies: Use base-ten blocks (tens rods and ones cubes) exclusively for 2-3 weeks before introducing symbolic representation.
Build numbers repeatedly: 'Show me 24 with your blocks' (2 rods and 4 cubes).
Use ten-frames for understanding grouping by 10.
accommodations: Allow extended time with concrete materials before moving to representational.
Use large, clearly labeled tens and ones cards to build numbers.
Practice with numbers 10-30 before extending beyond.

strategies: Use bilingual place-value charts if available.
Label tens rods and ones cubes with both numerical values and words.
Create visual anchor charts showing 'ten ones = one ten' with pictures.
Introduce regrouping: 'Can you show 24 a different way?' (1 ten and 14 ones).
Use place-value spinners to create and decompose random numbers.
Extend to numbers up to 100 using place-value language.
connections: Connect to skip-counting: 'How many ones in 3 tens?'
Apply to money: use dimes and pennies as concrete tens and ones.

strategies: Use open number lines with tens jumps: 24 + 13 becomes jumps of 10, 10, 3.
Provide base-ten block diagrams to support place-value strategies.
Teach one strategy thoroughly before introducing alternatives.
accommodations: Provide printed open number lines for students to use.
Allow use of base-ten blocks for checking answers.
Keep problems within 20 or 30 before extending.

strategies: Use bilingual problem mats showing 'start,' 'add/take away,' 'result.'
Provide bar model diagrams (tape diagrams) with visual support.
Create anchor charts showing both addition and subtraction visually.
sentenceFrames: I started with ___ and added/took away ___. Now I have ___.

strategies: Solve problems with two-step operations: 'Start with 24, add 10, then subtract 5.'
Work with larger numbers (up to 100) and multi-digit addition/subtraction.
Create and solve word problems for peers.
connections: Connect addition to skip-counting and arrays (repeated addition).
Use contexts like shopping, cooking, and classroom scenarios.

strategies: Provide shapes in multiple orientations and emphasize that rotation doesn't change the shape.
Use paper folding to create equal parts (halves, fourths) rather than drawing.
Focus on halves and fourths before exploring thirds, sixths, eighths.
accommodations: Use oversized, manipulatable shapes for easier handling.
Provide pre-divided shapes for students to color rather than requiring them to divide.
Allow extended time to explore shape composition and decomposition.

strategies: Create visual anchor charts showing shapes in many orientations.
Use bilingual shape and fraction vocabulary posters.
Provide shape-sorting and attribute games with clear visual examples.
sentenceFrames: This shape is divided into ___ equal parts.

strategies: Challenge students to divide shapes into equal parts using different methods (folding, drawing, cutting).
Explore relationships between fractions: 'Can 1/2 also be written as 2/4?'
Compose new shapes from given fractions.
connections: Connect to real-world shapes: pizza slices, sandwiches cut diagonally, etc.
Use fraction bars and circles for repeated practice.

strategies: Teach unit conversions using reference materials (12 inches in a foot, 100 cents in a dollar).
Use real or high-quality replica coins for coin-counting activities.
Teach o'clock and half-past times; introduce 15-minute intervals later.
accommodations: Provide conversion charts on desk references.
Use large analog clocks for demonstration and practice.
Practice coin counting with only pennies and dimes before introducing all coins.

strategies: Use bilingual measurement and time vocabulary posters.
Provide visual conversion charts (e.g., 1 foot = 12 inches with pictures).
Use consistent language and physical demonstrations.
sentenceFrames: I have ___ cents, which is ___ dollars.

strategies: Introduce elapsed time: 'If practice starts at 2:00 and ends at 3:30, how long was it?'
Work with all coin combinations without visual references.
Make and solve real-world problems involving money and measurement.
connections: Use measurement in cooking: follow recipes and measure ingredients.
Relate coin values to real purchases (e.g., 'How many pencils can you buy for 50 cents?').

strategies: Provide scaled picture graphs with clear scale notation (e.g., '1 = 2 items').
Use pre-made graph templates with labeled axes.
Limit scales to 1s, 2s, and 5s initially.
accommodations: Provide a scale reference card that students can use while reading graphs.
Use simple bar graphs before introducing scaled picture graphs.
Limit data to 3-4 categories.

strategies: Create bilingual graph titles and labels.
Use visual anchor charts showing how to use scales.
Provide data-collection templates with bilingual instructions.
sentenceFrames: ___ has ___ votes, which is ___ more than ___.

strategies: Create line plots with measurements (heights, lengths).
Use scales like 1 = 10 or 1 = 5.
Write questions based on graph data and answer them using arithmetic.
connections: Collect measurement data (plant heights, shoe sizes) and display on line plots.
Create multiple graph types for the same data to compare usefulness.

strategies: Use base-ten blocks (hundreds flats, tens rods, ones cubes) extensively.
Practice bundling: 'Make 10 ones into 1 ten. Make 10 tens into 1 hundred.'
Use place-value charts and have students build numbers frequently.
accommodations: Limit numbers to 0-100 before extending to 1000.
Provide place-value charts with visual labels (H, T, O).
Use highlighters to mark the hundreds, tens, and ones places distinctly.

strategies: Create bilingual place-value references with pictures and words.
Use color-coded place-value mats (red for hundreds, blue for tens, green for ones).
Provide number line posters from 0 to 1000 (with major intervals labeled).
Introduce flexible decomposition: Show 245 as (2 hundreds + 4 tens + 5 ones) OR (24 tens + 5 ones) OR (245 ones).
Create and compare numbers beyond 1000.
Solve place-value puzzles and riddles.
connections: Use money: dimes = tens, dollars = hundreds.
Relate place value to skip-counting by 10s and 100s.

strategies: Use concrete fraction models: pizza pieces, fraction circles, fraction bars.
Emphasize 'equal parts' repeatedly when introducing fractions.
Focus on unit fractions (1/2, 1/3, 1/4) before non-unit fractions.
accommodations: Provide pre-drawn shapes divided into equal parts for coloring.
Use fold-and-cut activities to create physical equal parts.
Allow extended use of manipulatives before moving to pictorial representations.

strategies: Create bilingual fraction vocabulary: half, third, fourth, etc., with visuals.
Use real objects divided into equal parts (real pizzas, real fruit cut into halves).
Provide anchor charts showing fractions with clear visual representations.
sentenceFrames: This shape is divided into ___ equal parts.

strategies: Introduce equivalent fractions using visual models: 1/2 = 2/4.
Compare fractions with the same denominator and same numerator.
Place fractions on number lines to deepen understanding.
connections: Connect to real-world contexts: sharing food, cutting rope, measuring ingredients.
Use fraction kits and bar models for repeated practice.

strategies: Use area models with graph paper: draw rectangles and count grid squares.
Teach partial products: 23 × 4 = (20 × 4) + (3 × 4) = 80 + 12 = 92.
Use base-ten blocks to model two-digit multiplication.
accommodations: Provide pre-drawn area models with partially filled dimensions.
Limit to multiplication by single digits before extending.
Allow use of multiplication chart for reference.

strategies: Use bilingual multiplication vocabulary and anchor charts.
Provide step-by-step visual models for area and partial products.
Create sentence frames for explaining multiplication strategies.
Work with larger two-digit × two-digit multiplication.
Solve division with remainders and interpret them in context.
Connect multiplication and division strategies.
connections: Create word problems using real contexts (shopping, cooking, arrays).
Solve problems where remainders have meaning: 'If 23 cookies go in boxes of 4, how many full boxes? How many cookies are left over?'

strategies: Cover shapes with square tiles and count them to find area.
Walk around perimeter with tape or string and measure to find distance around.
Use graph paper with shapes already drawn; students count and record.
accommodations: Provide grid paper with unit squares clearly marked.
Limit to rectangles before exploring irregular shapes.
Allow extended practice counting before introducing formulas.

strategies: Use bilingual vocabulary cards for area and perimeter (with pictures).
Provide anchor charts showing 'inside' (area) vs. 'around' (perimeter).
Label square units and linear units distinctly.
Introduce area and perimeter formulas after extensive counting practice.
Explore shapes with the same area but different perimeters.
Solve problems in real contexts (finding area of classroom, garden, etc.).
connections: Use centimeter or inch grid paper for all initial work.
Connect to real-world measurement: 'How much carpet covers our reading area? How much tape goes around the border?'

strategies: Sort shapes by obvious attributes (color, size) before geometric properties.
Use Venn diagrams and attribute games with clear visual models.
Provide shapes in multiple orientations to build robust understanding.
accommodations: Limit to sorting by one attribute at a time.
Use real classroom objects to find shapes before abstract work.
Allow extended exploratory time with shape blocks.

strategies: Create bilingual shape name and property vocabulary charts.
Use picture cards showing shape examples in multiple languages.
Provide sorting mats with pictured examples.
Explore hierarchical relationships: all squares are rectangles, but not all rectangles are squares.
Identify shapes in the real world and classify them.
Use properties to create shape puzzles and riddles.
connections: Connect shape classification to architecture and design.
Explore symmetry and transformations of shapes.

strategies: Use fraction bars and area models for all addition/subtraction.
Find common denominators using visual equivalence models, then move to LCM.
Work only with unit and like denominators initially.
accommodations: Provide pre-made fraction strips showing equivalences.
Use denominators 2, 4, 8 and 3, 6 initially (easier common denominators).
Allow calculator verification of simplified fractions.

strategies: Use bilingual fraction operation vocabulary and step-by-step anchor charts.
Show real-world contexts (recipes, measurements) alongside symbolic problems.
Provide vocabulary cards and sentence frames for explaining steps.
sentenceFrames: I found the LCD of ___ and ___.

strategies: Work with unlike denominators and less familiar denominators.
Solve multi-step fraction problems and word problems.
Explore why common denominators are necessary.
connections: Use measurement and cooking contexts: 'Combine 2/3 cup milk and 1/4 cup cream.'
Create real-world problems and solve them.

strategies: Use hundredths grids to represent decimal tenths and hundredths.
Explicitly connect fractions to decimals: 1/10 = 0.1, 1/100 = 0.01.
Use money (dollars and cents) as a concrete decimal representation.
accommodations: Provide hundredths grids for all decimal comparisons and representations.
Use color-coded place-value charts showing tenths and hundredths.
Allow extended practice with decimal representations before comparisons.

strategies: Use bilingual decimal vocabulary and place-value charts.
Connect decimals to money, which may be more familiar.
Provide visual anchor charts showing decimal-to-fraction conversions.
Work with decimals to the thousandths place.
Solve comparison problems: 'Which is greater, 0.3 or 0.25?'
Connect decimals to measurements (0.5 m, 0.25 kg).
connections: Use money for all decimal work: comparing prices, making change, etc.
Connect to fractional amounts: 'If milk costs $3.50 per gallon, what is 0.5 gallons?'

strategies: Build rectangles on grid paper and count squares before introducing formulas.
Use real or drawn rectangles with labels for length and width.
Distinguish area (square units) from perimeter (units) with visual labels.
accommodations: Provide grid paper for all area calculations.
Use pre-labeled rectangles with all dimensions provided.
Allow calculator use for checking multiplication in area formulas.

strategies: Create bilingual measurement vocabulary and formula cards.
Use visual anchors showing the difference between area and perimeter.
Provide step-by-step instruction cards with pictures and words.
Solve problems finding a missing dimension given area and one dimension.
Convert between measurement units (cm to m, inches to feet).
Apply area and perimeter to real-world design problems.
connections: Measure and find area/perimeter of classroom objects and spaces.
Solve problems like: 'If you have 20 meters of fencing, what are the possible dimensions of a rectangular garden?'

strategies: Use physical tools: pencils, straws, arms to demonstrate lines, angles, and rays.
Define and model parallel and perpendicular lines with real-world examples.
Use corner checking tools to identify right angles.
accommodations: Provide anchor charts with clear definitions and examples of each type of line and angle.
Use manipulatives that can be repositioned to explore angles.
Allow extended exploration before formal naming.

strategies: Create bilingual anchor charts with pictures for parallel, perpendicular, acute, obtuse, right angles.
Use action words: point (for vertex), stand up (for perpendicular), run together (for parallel).
Provide visual vocabulary cards with examples.
Explore lines of symmetry in various shapes and letters.
Classify triangles by angles and sides.
Identify parallel, perpendicular, and intersecting lines in architecture.
connections: Find examples of parallel and perpendicular lines in the classroom and building.
Explore symmetry in art, letters, and nature.

strategies: Use area models exclusively: draw rectangles and shade to show multiplication.
Start with 'of' problems: 'What is 1/2 of 1/2?'
Use concrete fraction strips overlapped to model multiplication.
accommodations: Provide pre-drawn area models and grids for students to shade.
Limit to multiplying by unit fractions initially.
Allow extended practice with models before introducing the algorithm.

strategies: Use bilingual area model anchor charts with step-by-step instructions.
Connect 'of' explicitly to multiplication with visual examples.
Provide fraction multiplication vocabulary cards and sentence frames.
Work with non-unit fractions and mixed numbers.
Explore why multiplying by a fraction less than 1 gives a smaller result.
Solve complex fraction multiplication problems in real contexts.
connections: Use cooking/recipe contexts: 'If a recipe makes 4 servings and calls for 3/4 cup milk, how much for 1 serving?'
Explore scaling: 'If a recipe is scaled to 1/2 size, what is 1/2 of 3/4 cup?'

strategies: Use number lines to show 'how many groups': 'How many 1/2s fit in 3?'
Use concrete models (cutting paper, breaking objects) to show division.
Focus on dividing by unit fractions before general fractions.
accommodations: Provide number line templates with divisions already marked.
Use real objects that can be cut or divided physically.
Allow extended exploration of models before introducing the algorithm.

strategies: Use bilingual number line anchor charts and step-by-step instructions.
Create visual examples showing 'how many ___ fit in ___?'
Provide vocabulary cards for division of fractions.
Work with general fractions and mixed numbers.
Explore why the 'invert and multiply' rule works using models.
Solve complex real-world division of fraction problems.
connections: Use contexts like 'A board is 6 feet long. I need 1/4-foot pieces. How many?'
Explore scaling and recipe problems: 'If 1/2 cup makes 4 servings, how many servings does 2 cups make?'

strategies: Use hundredths grids for visual representations of decimal multiplication.
Teach decimal multiplication by first multiplying as whole numbers, then placing the decimal.
Use money as a concrete decimal context for all operations.
accommodations: Provide step-by-step cards showing how to multiply and place decimals.
Use calculators to check answers, focusing on reasonableness.
Limit to multiplication by single-digit numbers initially.

strategies: Use bilingual decimal operation anchor charts and vocabulary cards.
Show money problems alongside symbolic decimal problems.
Provide visual step-by-step instructions for each operation.
sentenceFrames: I multiplied ___ × ___, which gave me ___. Then I placed the decimal ___.

strategies: Work with division of decimals and placement of quotient decimals.
Solve multi-step decimal problems.
Apply decimals to measurement and real-world contexts.
connections: Use shopping contexts: 'If apples cost $1.25 per pound, what do 3 pounds cost?'
Explore measurement: 'A board is 2.5 meters long. If cut into 5 equal pieces, how long is each?'

strategies: Teach PEMDAS with visuals: color-code each operation type.
Use step-by-step cards showing how to evaluate expressions.
Practice with one operation at a time before combining.
accommodations: Provide expression evaluation templates with steps outlined.
Use calculators to verify answers after solving by hand.
Limit to expressions without exponents and nested parentheses initially.

strategies: Use bilingual PEMDAS/BODMAS anchor charts with color-coding.
Provide step-by-step vocabulary and examples in both languages.
Create visual mnemonics (like pictures for the order).
sentenceFrames: First I do the ___ in the parentheses.

strategies: Work with expressions including exponents and multiple parentheses.
Explore the distributive property and equivalent expressions.
Create and solve complex multi-step expressions.
connections: Use contexts like: 'Order matters! Show why (2 + 3) × 4 is different from 2 + 3 × 4.'
Apply to real-world problems: 'Calculate the cost: (5 × $2.50) + (3 × $1.75).'

strategies: Build rectangular prisms with unit cubes and count the total.
Show layers and help students understand V = (base area) × height.
Use grid paper diagrams with dimension labels.
accommodations: Provide pre-drawn rectangular prisms with grid paper showing layers.
Use physical unit cubes for all initial investigations.
Limit to simple whole-number dimensions initially.

strategies: Use bilingual volume vocabulary and anchor charts (length, width, height, cubic units).
Show step-by-step visual instructions for finding volume.
Provide vocabulary cards and sentence frames.
sentenceFrames: The base area is ___. The height is ___. The volume is ___.

strategies: Solve for missing dimensions given volume.
Work with composite shapes (adding/subtracting volumes).
Apply to real-world containers and spaces.
connections: Calculate volumes of classroom objects and spaces.
Solve problems: 'If a box holds 240 cubic inches and is 6 inches tall, what is the base area?'

strategies: Use large coordinate grids with visuals showing 'right first, then up.'
Plot one point at a time with teacher guidance.
Use colored axes and clear axis labels.
accommodations: Provide coordinate plane templates with axes pre-drawn and labeled.
Start with small coordinates (0-5) before extending.
Allow extended practice plotting before reading or identifying patterns.

strategies: Use bilingual coordinate vocabulary (x-axis, y-axis, ordered pair) with visuals.
Show step-by-step instructions for plotting points in both languages.
Create anchor charts showing how to read and write ordered pairs.
sentenceFrames: The point (___, ___) is ___ units right and ___ units up.
I plot (x, y) by going ___ and then ___.

strategies: Identify and describe patterns from plotted points.
Calculate distances between points using coordinate differences.
Explore relationships between x and y coordinates.
connections: Create classroom maps with coordinates for key locations.
Plot real data (temperature by day, height vs. age) and analyze patterns.

strategies: Use fraction strips and area models to compare visual sizes
Number line activities with physical movement along lines marked with fractions
Concrete objects: compare pile sizes of halves, thirds, fourths
accommodations: Allow continued use of manipulatives while developing abstract thinking
Provide pre-marked number lines for plotting
Use larger fonts on number lines and fraction models

strategies: Dual-language fraction vocabulary cards (English/home language)
Visual anchor chart: 'Greater than = more/bigger pieces'
Gesture along number line while naming fractions
sentenceFrames: On the number line, ___ is to the ___ of ___.
The denominator tells us ___, and the numerator tells us ___.

strategies: Investigate relationships: how many halves in 3/4? Generate equivalent fractions between 1/3 and 1/2
Explore density property: infinite fractions exist between any two fractions
Connect to ratios: what does comparing numbers mean in real-world contexts (population, measurements)?
connections: Music: note durations as fractions (whole, half, quarter notes)
Cooking: fraction equivalence when scaling recipes
Measurement: comparing lengths in different units (inches, feet, fractions)

strategies: Decimal grids (10×10) to show 3/4 as 75 squares and 0.75
Place-value charts: tenths place, hundredths place for decimals
Concrete: use coins and bills to connect money (0.75 = 75 cents) to decimals
accommodations: Provide pre-filled equivalence tables for frequent fractions/decimals/percents
Use color-coded manipulatives for different representations
Allow reference cards during initial practice

strategies: Visual conversion flowcharts (Fraction → Decimal → Percent)
Comparison of how different languages express fractions
Pairs: one student reads aloud fraction, partner writes decimal
sentenceFrames: ___ as a fraction is ___. As a decimal, it is ___. As a percent, it is ___.

strategies: Investigate repeating decimals (1/3 = 0.333...); why does this happen?
Explore percent errors in real data (survey results, measurements)
Justify which representation is most useful in different contexts
connections: Science: concentration of solutions using percent
Data: reporting survey results using different formats

strategies: Concrete: arrays of objects (12 as 3×4, 4×3, 2×6) to visualize factors
Prime factorization trees as visual organizers
Use factor blocks (base-ten-like) to build rectangles showing factors
accommodations: Provide factor lists for checking work initially
Highlight common factors in color on pre-made lists
Allow use of multiplication tables

strategies: Anchor chart with prime vs composite definitions in English and visuals
Pair ELL students for factor-finding games (L1 discussions then L2 reporting)
Word wall: prime, composite, factor, multiple, GCF, LCM with pictures
sentenceFrames: ___ is prime because it has only ___ and ___ as factors.

strategies: Investigate patterns in prime numbers (twin primes, Goldbach conjecture)
Real-world application: find GCF to split items equally; LCM for scheduling repetitions
Explore Euclid's algorithm for finding GCF efficiently
connections: Music: repeating patterns and LCM (beats, measures)

strategies: Concrete ratios: cubes in two colors (3 red, 2 blue = 3:2 ratio)
Double number lines for ratios and rates
Visual tape diagrams showing ratio relationships
accommodations: Provide pre-made double number lines for guided practice
Use consistent color-coding (one color per quantity)
Start with simple whole-number ratios

strategies: Visual models of ratio vs rate (diagram of part:part vs. unit rate)
Language support: 'ratio means... rate means...' with examples
Gesture and movement: 'For every 3..., there are 2...' with hand motions
Explore cross-multiplication in proportion problems; why does it work?
Scale recipes, maps, models using ratios
Investigate non-proportional relationships (when ratios change)
connections: Maps: scale factor from map to reality

strategies: Area models for fraction multiplication (rectangle with two dimensions marked)
Repeated addition for 3 × 1/4 (visual or concrete)
Paper folding and shading for visual division (1/2 of 3/4)
accommodations: Provide blank area model grids for structuring work
Allow manipulatives (paper strips, rods) throughout
Explicit step-by-step templates: numerator × numerator, denominator × denominator

strategies: Picture cards: 'of' means multiply in word problems (1/2 of 10)
Bilingual math vocabulary: 'numerator,' 'denominator,' 'reciprocal'
Real-world context initially in familiar language, then English
sentenceFrames: To divide by ___, I multiply by ___.

strategies: Investigate why multiplying by fractions < 1 makes smaller results
Verify distributive property with fraction examples
Explore complex fractions and simplification
Percent grids (10×10) to shade and count squares for visual percent
Concrete money contexts (25¢ of $1 = 25%)
Area models divided into 100 equal parts
accommodations: Provide 10×10 grids pre-drawn for practice
Use repeated subtraction context if needed
Allow calculator use to focus on concept vs. computation

strategies: Percent symbol explanation: 'per 100' = cent (100 in other languages too)
Parallel examples: English and home language percent descriptions
Visual anchor: 'percent means ___ out of 100'
Explore percent increase/decrease; why are 10% of different amounts different?
Compound percent problems (consecutive discounts)
Percent of percent situations
connections: Growth: population percent change year to year

strategies: Read-aloud word problems with teacher modeling underline-the-question strategy
Visual graphic organizers: What do I know? What am I finding?
Concrete manipulatives for acting out scenarios
accommodations: Simplified word problems with fewer irrelevant details initially
Provide word banks for problem-solving vocabulary
Use number lines and hundreds charts as visible tools

strategies: Pre-teach math vocabulary before problem-solving lessons
Picture-based problems before text-heavy ones
Think-pair-share in small groups with sentence frames
sentenceFrames: My answer is about ___, which makes sense because ___.

strategies: Multi-step problems requiring planning and strategy selection
Open-ended problems with multiple solution paths
Error analysis: identify and explain mistakes in given solutions
connections: Project planning: estimate time/cost for real activities
Recipes: estimate quantities; adjust if needed

strategies: Use letters in context (n for number of items, t for time) from start
Number lines with 'x' as unknown location
Concrete: bags of unknown items (x) vs known piles
accommodations: Consistent variable names (x for unknown, not random letters initially)
Show variable as standing for a specific number each time
Use balance scales or equal-sign concepts concretely

strategies: Explain variable as 'mystery number' or 'stand-in for a number'
Connect variable to real-world unknowns (age, height, cost)
Visual: x = □ (variable is a box representing a number)
Explore variables in formulas (A = lw for area)
Investigate how changing variable value changes expression value
Create own word problems with given variable expressions
Concrete arrays/groups to show commutative property (5 groups of 3 = 3 groups of 5)
Parentheses with physical groupings (blocks stacked in piles)
Area models for distributive property (split rectangle into subareas)
accommodations: Color-coding for like terms in algebraic expressions
Sentence frames for naming properties
Provide property reference card for checking work

strategies: Property names translated to home language when possible
Show non-examples (what is NOT commutative?) alongside examples
Gesture and movement: rearrange items to show commutativity
sentenceFrames: I can rewrite this as ___ using the ___ property.

strategies: Prove properties algebraically; why are they always true?
Explore which properties apply to subtraction/division (not all do)
Use properties to justify efficient mental math strategies
connections: Efficient computation: using commutative property to compute easy order
Geometry: using properties to calculate equivalent areas

strategies: Bar models/tape diagrams showing equation structure
Balance scales (real or drawings) for 'doing same to both sides'
Visual equation representations: □ + 5 = 12
accommodations: Step-by-step templates: subtract from both sides, divide both sides
Allow continued use of objects or drawings to verify solutions
Provide inverse-operation reminders

strategies: Bilingual anchor chart: 'add ↔ subtract' and 'multiply ↔ divide'
Gesture and narration: 'We subtract 5 from here (left), so we subtract 5 from here (right)'
Visual inverse operation cards
sentenceFrames: I check my answer: ___ + ___ = ___, which is true.

strategies: Two-step equations; choosing most efficient solving order
Equations with variables on both sides
Create real-world contexts for given equations; justify solutions
connections: Budgeting: solve for cost given constraints
Recipes: solve for serving size or ingredient amount
Distance: solve for time or speed

strategies: Concrete: cover shapes with unit squares to count area
Decompose shapes into rectangles; find total area
Use rope/string for perimeter; count around
accommodations: Provide grid paper with visible unit squares
Highlight perimeter path with color
Provide formulas on reference sheets without penalty

strategies: Visual vocabulary: perimeter (around), area (inside), volume (fills)
Physical demonstration: walk around shape's perimeter, point inside for area
Label dimensions clearly with units
Find dimensions given area and perimeter constraints
Surface area of prisms; relate to wrapping boxes
Investigate relationship between area and perimeter (can have same area, different perimeter)
connections: Fencing costs: calculate fencing needed (perimeter) vs ground covered (area)
Painting: calculate surface area of walls or prisms

strategies: Use physical angle models (straws, rulers) to show angle concepts
Protractor introduction with large, colored angles first
Create angles by rotating ray; measure rotation
accommodations: Use pre-marked angle templates for practice
Large protractors with clear degree markings
Color-code angle pairs (vertical angles same color)

strategies: Bilingual angle vocabulary: acute (pequeño), right (recto), obtuse (grande)
Show ray motion with hand gestures while naming angles
Anchor chart with angle classifications and examples
sentenceFrames: The measure of this angle is ___ degrees.

strategies: Investigate angle relationships (linear pairs, adjacent angles)
Explore angles in real-world contexts (roof angles, ramp angles)
Prove vertical angles are equal using properties
connections: Engineering: ramp inclination for wheelchair accessibility

strategies: Cut out and measure angles of shapes; sum by combining
Draw and color code interior vs exterior angles
Use dynamic geometry software to explore angle sums
accommodations: Provide angle sum formulas on references initially
Use pre-cut shapes for summing angles
Allow calculation of individual angles before summing

strategies: Interior vs exterior demonstrated visually with pointers
Anchor chart with angle sum formulas and pictures of each polygon
Practice saying: 'Triangle angles sum to 180 degrees'
sentenceFrames: The sum of interior angles in a ___ is ___.
Each angle in a regular ___ measures ___.

strategies: Derive angle sum formula algebraically using triangulation
Explore regular polygons; angle relationships in regular figures
Exterior angle theorem: exterior angle = sum of remote interior angles
connections: Art: tessellations (why certain angles fit together)
Engineering: structural design using angle properties
Nature: polygon appearances in honeycombs, crystals

strategies: Use actual objects to measure and compare (yard stick, meter stick)
Unit conversion chart with visual size comparisons
Start with familiar conversions (12 inches = 1 foot)
accommodations: Provide unit conversion references without penalty
Use proportions explicitly set up: 1 ft : 12 in = ? ft : ? in
Allow hands-on measurement practice

strategies: Measurement vocabulary in both languages (pulgada/inch, pie/foot)
Visual size comparisons side-by-side
Practice sentences: '1 foot equals 12 inches'
Metric and customary system comparisons; when use each?
Derived conversions from basic units using proportions
Precision in measurement; significant figures concept introduction
connections: Science: measure in appropriate units (mm for small, m for distances)
Cooking: convert recipe measurements between systems
Medicine: drug dosages in mg/kg conversions

strategies: Physical experiments: roll dice, spin spinners, draw cards
List outcomes concretely on cards to move and organize
Systematic listing through organized tables
